lines.text: Chapter one, section two. What is a Strategy?
In everyday conversation, the word 'strategy' gets used loosely — someone might say their strategy is to play aggressively, or to be patient, or to trap their opponents.
In game theory, though, the word has a much more precise and demanding definition.
Wikipedia captures it well: a strategy is a complete algorithm for playing the game, meaning it specifies exactly what a player will do in every possible situation that could arise during the game.
Not a general approach, not a rough style of play, but a specific, exhaustive prescription covering every possible situation without exception.
To appreciate how demanding this definition is, think about what a complete poker strategy would actually require.
It's not enough to say you play tight and aggressive before the flop.

A complete strategy needs to say exactly which hands you raise with, which you call with, and which you fold — and in what proportions — from every position, at every table size, against every possible betting action you might face.
And then it needs to describe all of that for every possible flop, every possible turn card, every possible river card, every possible action your opponents might have taken on every street.
Brokos gives a concrete illustration: imagine his pre-flop strategy when sitting first to act at a nine-handed table.
He might raise three times the big blind with any pair of sevens or better, with ace-queen offsuit or better, and with any suited broadway hand.

For suited connectors ranging from ten-nine suited down to seven-six suited, he might raise only twenty percent of the time — meaning four times out of five he folds that hand in that spot, and one time out of five he raises.
He would fold everything else.

But even that level of detail is only the beginning, because a complete strategy would also need to describe how he responds to a three-bet of various sizes from each of his opponents — a different answer potentially for every opponent, every three-bet size, and every hand in his range.
The sheer volume of information required for a genuinely complete strategy is staggering, far beyond what any human being can hold in their head at once.

This is one of the central reasons why computers are so valuable for studying game theory in a rigorous way: the machines can store and process the kind of exhaustive strategic detail that would completely overwhelm a human mind.
Now, when we're actually sitting at a poker table, we're not going to be operating from anything close to a complete strategy in this formal sense.
We make decisions situation by situation, drawing on pattern recognition, intuition, and whatever principles we've internalized from study.
But here's the key insight from the book: even if we can't hold a complete strategy in our heads, we still need to think beyond the current moment.
To make a good decision right now, we often need to consider what situations we might face later in the hand, and how we intend to respond to those situations.

For example, if you're deciding whether to call a bet on the turn, part of that decision involves thinking about what you'll do on the river — what kinds of bets you'll be able to make, what kinds of bets you'll be able to call, what hands in your range can serve what purpose.

Similarly, having some model of your opponent's likely strategy — what range of hands they might have, what they tend to do with strong hands versus weak ones — is essential for making good decisions yourself.
Knowing that a particular river card would leave you vulnerable to bluffs, for instance, might influence which hands you call with on the turn.
So even without a fully explicit strategy, you're always implicitly working with strategic frameworks, both for yourself and for your opponents.
The game-theoretic definition just makes all of that explicit and rigorous.
